result.markdown:
# Workflow optimisation using a Data Independent Acquisition spike-in study


::: {.cell}

:::


DIA-NN provides multiple quantifications, e.g. derived from the MS1 or MS2 spectra, and at precursor or protein (protein group) level. The term 'precursor' refers to a charged peptide species and is the basic unit of identification and quantification in DIA. Hence, in the context of DIA we refer to a precursor table, instead of to a PSM table in DDA. 

Examples of different quantities are:

- raw MS1 area: Ms1.Area, normalised MS1 Area: Ms1.Normalised, MS2 Precursor quantities: Precursor.Quantity, Normalised MS2 Precursor quantities: Precursor.Normalised, etc., which are all at the precursor level 
- MS2 based summary at the protein (protein group)-level: PG.MaxLFQ

Here, we will use the `Precursor.Quantity`, `Precursor.Normalised` and `PG.MaxLFQ` column.

## Experimental context 

[@VanPuyveldeEtal2026] made a benchmark dataset where they mixed Ecoli, yeast and human digested proteins in three different ratio's, i.e. Mix A: 5% E. coli, 30% Yeast, 65% Human, Mix B: 20% E. coli, 15% Yeast and 65% Human, Mix C: 32% E. Coli, 3% Yeast and 65% Human. 

Each mixture was analyzed 6 times on an Thermo Orbitrap Astral mass spectrometer. 

# Load packages

We load the `msqrob2` package, along with additional packages for
data manipulation and visualisation.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library("QFeatures")  
library("dplyr") 
library("tidyr")
library("ggplot2")
library("msqrob2")    
library("stringr")
library("ExploreModelMatrix")
library("MsCoreUtils") 
library("matrixStats")
library("patchwork")
library("kableExtra")
library("ComplexHeatmap")
library("purrr")
library("tibble")
library("scater")
```
:::


# Data

## Precursor table

We load the output from DIA-NN parquet file.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
precursorFile <- "data/speciesmix-vanpuyvelde2026.parquet" 
```
:::



We can import the report.parquet file using the `read_parquet` function from the `arrow` package. 
Note, that older versions of DIA-NN store the output as report.tsv. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
precursors <- arrow::read_parquet(precursorFile) # function from the arrow package
#precursors <- data.table::fread(precursorFile) # For older versions of DIA-NN, where the results are stored as tsv files. Note that the precursorFile then would point to "report.tsv" 
```
:::


We filter the data to reduce the memory footprint. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
precursors <- precursors |> 
  select(
         Run, 
         Precursor.Id, 
         Modified.Sequence, 
         Stripped.Sequence, 
         Precursor.Charge, 
         Protein.Group, 
         Protein.Names,
         Protein.Ids,
         Genes,
         Precursor.Quantity,
         Precursor.Normalised,
         Ms1.Area,
         Ms1.Normalised,
         PG.MaxLFQ,
         Q.Value, 
         Lib.Q.Value,
         PG.Q.Value,
         Lib.PG.Q.Value,
         Proteotypic,
         Decoy, # Not available in older versions of DIA-NN
         RT)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
precursors <- precursors |> 
  mutate(species = Protein.Names |> 
           strsplit(split="_") |> 
           sapply(`[`, 2) |>
            substr(start=1,stop=5))
precursors |> 
  pull(species) |>
  table()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

  BOVIN   ECOLI   HUMAN   MOUSE     PIG   PSEAI   SCVLA   SHEEP   YEAST 
  19131  145236 1639857      18     155     434     286      18  366318 
```


:::
:::


We only keep ECOLI, HUMAN and YEAST. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
precursors <- precursors |> 
  filter(species %in% c("ECOLI", "HUMAN", "YEAST"))
precursors |> 
  pull(species) |>
  table()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

  ECOLI   HUMAN   YEAST 
 145236 1639857  366318 
```


:::
:::


## Sample annotation table

The [sample annotation table](#sec-annotation_table)) is not available
and can be generated from the run labels, as the researchers included information on the design in the filenames.

We will make a new data frame with the annotation. 

1. We first generate variable `runCol`, that gives an overview of the different runs in the experiment, i.e. the unique names in the column Run of the report file. This is a mandatory column in the annotation file that is required by the `readQFeatures` function that will be used to generate a QFeatures object with the quant data. 

2. Next we generate variable `sampleId`, to pinpoint the different samples in the dataset. This can be extracted from the run names as the researchers have stored information on the meta data in the file names for each run. E.g. for run LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP2 this is the condition, i.e. Condition_C, and the replicate, i.e. _REP2. 
    
We first replace the redundant pattern "LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_" with an empty character string. 

3. We make add the variable `condition` by parsing the variable sampleId and keeping the first letter of the string

4. We make add the variable `rep` by parsing the variable sampleId, and  

    a. splitting it according to pattern "_", 
    b. keeping the right part of the string (second element of the vector of strings in each list item of the substr output)
    c. converting it into a factor
    

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
annot <- data.frame(runCol = precursors |> 
                       pull(Run) |> 
                       unique() # 1. 
                   ) |>
    mutate(sampleId = gsub(x = runCol, pattern = "LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_", replacement = ""), #2.
           condition =  substr(sampleId,1,1), #3.
           rep = sampleId |> 
               str_split("_") |> #4.a
               sapply(`[`, 2) |> #4.b
               as.factor()
           )
```
:::


## Convert to QFeatures

First, recall that the precursor table is file in long format. 
Every quantitative column in the precursor table contains
information for multiple runs. Therefore, the function split the table
based on the run identifier, given by the `runCol` argument (for
DIA-NN, that identifier is contained in `run`). 
So, the
`QFeatures` object after import will contain as many sets as there are
runs. 
Next, the function links the annotation table with the PSM data.
To achieve this, the annotation table must contain a `runCol` column
that provides the run identifier in which each sample has been
acquired, and this information will be used to match the identifiers
in the `Run` column of the precursor table. 

Here, we will use the `Precursor.Quantity` column as quantification input. 

Note, that the 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
(qf <- readQFeatures(assayData = precursors, 
                             colData = annot,
                             quantCols = "Precursor.Quantity",
                             runCol = "Run",
                             fnames = "Precursor.Id"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 18 sets:

 [1] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP1: SummarizedExperiment with 121273 rows and 1 columns 
 [2] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP2: SummarizedExperiment with 122253 rows and 1 columns 
 [3] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP3: SummarizedExperiment with 123044 rows and 1 columns 
 ...
 [16] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP4: SummarizedExperiment with 111527 rows and 1 columns 
 [17] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP5: SummarizedExperiment with 111506 rows and 1 columns 
 [18] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP6: SummarizedExperiment with 111405 rows and 1 columns 
```


:::
:::


# Data preprocessing{#sec-basic_preprocess}

The data preprocessing workflow for DIA data is similar to the workflow for DDA-LFQ data, but there are suble differences as we start from precursor level data and have additional columns. 

## Encoding missing values

We replace any zero in the quantitative data with an `NA`, which is needed for downstream log2 transformation.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf <- zeroIsNA(qf, names(qf))
```
:::


## Precursor Filtering 

Filtering removes low-quality and unreliable precursors that would otherwise introduce noise and artefacts in the data. 

### Remove questionable identifications


We apply standard filtering advised by DIA-NN: 

1. q-value threshold of 0.01 for the identification of precursors (`Q.Value`) and protein groups (`PG.Q.Value`). If the file is processed via matching between runs, it is also useful to filter on Lib.Q.Value and Lib.PG.Q.Value.

2. Remove precursors that could not be mapped, i.e. when `Precursor.Id` column is an empty string.  

3. Filter decoys, i.e. only keep precursors for which the `Decoy` column equals 0. (Note, that the `Decoy` column is not present in the output of older versions of DIA-NN)

4. Keeping only proteotypic peptides, which map uniquely to a specific protein.



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf <-  qf |>
  filterFeatures(~ Q.Value <= 0.01 & #1.
                     PG.Q.Value <= 0.01 &  #1.
                     Lib.Q.Value <= 0.01 & #1.
                     Lib.PG.Q.Value <= 0.01 & #1.
                     Precursor.Id != "" & #2.
                     Decoy == 0 & #3. (Note, that this filter is not available with previous versions of DIA-NN, as the report.tsv file did not include a Decoy column. So other strategies are needed if Decoys are in the output file.)
                     Proteotypic == 1) #4.
```
:::


### Assay joining

Up to now, the data from different runs were kept in separate assays. We can now join the sets into a precursor set using joinAssays(). Sets are joined by stacking the columns (samples) in a matrix and rows (features) are matched according to a row identifier, here the `Precursor.Id`.
We will store the result in the assay with name: `precursors`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
(qf <- joinAssays(
  x = qf,
  i = names(qf),
  fcol = "Precursor.Id",
  name = "precursors"
  ))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 19 sets:

 [1] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP1: SummarizedExperiment with 114429 rows and 1 columns 
 [2] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP2: SummarizedExperiment with 115386 rows and 1 columns 
 [3] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP3: SummarizedExperiment with 116113 rows and 1 columns 
 ...
 [17] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP5: SummarizedExperiment with 104760 rows and 1 columns 
 [18] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP6: SummarizedExperiment with 104709 rows and 1 columns 
 [19] precursors: SummarizedExperiment with 143938 rows and 18 columns 
```


:::
:::


### Filtering: Remove highly missing precursors

We keep peptides that were observed at last 9 times out of the $n
= 18$ samples, so that we can estimate the peptide characteristics. 
We tolerate the following proportion of NAs:
$\text{pNA} = \frac{(n - 9)}{n} = 0.50$, so we keep peptides that
are observed in at least 50% of the samples. This is an arbitrary
value that may need to be adjusted depending on the experiment and the
data set.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nObs <- 9
n <- ncol(qf[["precursors"]])

(qf <- filterNA(qf, i = "precursors", pNA = (n - nObs) / n))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 19 sets:

 [1] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP1: SummarizedExperiment with 114429 rows and 1 columns 
 [2] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP2: SummarizedExperiment with 115386 rows and 1 columns 
 [3] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP3: SummarizedExperiment with 116113 rows and 1 columns 
 ...
 [17] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP5: SummarizedExperiment with 104760 rows and 1 columns 
 [18] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP6: SummarizedExperiment with 104709 rows and 1 columns 
 [19] precursors: SummarizedExperiment with 121396 rows and 18 columns 
```


:::
:::



### Filter one-hit wonders

Here, we remove proteins that can only be found by one peptide, as such proteins may not be trustworthy.

1. We first calculate how many distinct peptides map to each protein (`Protein.id`). We use the stripped precursor sequence, i.e. sequence of the base peptide for this purpose. 

2. We store this information in the row data of the precursors assay

3. We filter precursors of one-hit wonder proteins.  


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Filter for peptides per protein
pepsPerProtDf <- qf[["precursors"]] |> 
    rowData() |> 
    data.frame() |>
    dplyr::select("Stripped.Sequence", "Protein.Ids") |>
    group_by(Protein.Ids) |>
    mutate(pepsPerProt = Stripped.Sequence |>
               unique() |> length()
           ) #1.

rowData(qf[["precursors"]])$pepsPerProt <- pepsPerProtDf$pepsPerProt #2. 

qf <- filterFeatures(qf, 
                              ~ pepsPerProt > 1, 
                              keep = TRUE) #3.
```
:::


## Log-transformation 

We perform log2-transformation with `logTransform()` from the
`QFeatures` package. We use `base = 2` and store the result in a new summarized experiment named `precursors_log`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf <- logTransform(qf, 
                            base = 2, 
                            i = "precursors", 
                            name = "precursors_log")
```
:::


## Normalisation {#sec-norm}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf[, , "precursors_log"] |> #1.
  longForm(colvars = c( "rep", "condition")) |>  #2. 
  data.frame() |> 
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  ggplot() + #3.
  aes(x = value,
      colour = condition,
      group = colname) +
  geom_density() +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf[, , "precursors_log"] |> #1.
  longForm(colvars = c( "rep", "condition"), rowvars = "species") |>  #2. 
  data.frame() |> 
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  ggplot() + #3.
  aes(x = value,
      colour = condition,
      group = colname) +
  geom_density() +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(~species)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Note, that this is a very clean technical dataset, so no normalisation seems to be required. 
We also know the non differentially abundant features ("HUMAN"). 

Nevertheless, we will evaluate 

1. no normalisation, STATS = 0 (we do not change the intensities)
2. median normalisation, 
3. total loading normalisation, and
4. median of ratio normalisation 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf <- sweep( #4. Subtract log2 norm factor column-by-column (MARGIN = 2)
  qf,
  MARGIN = 2, 
  STATS = 0, #1.
  i = "precursors_log",
  name = "precursors_norm_no"
)

qf <- sweep( #4. Subtract log2 norm factor column-by-column (MARGIN = 2)
  qf,
  MARGIN = 2, 
  STATS = nf_log_med(qf, i="precursors_log"), #1.
  i = "precursors_log",
  name = "precursors_norm_med"
)

qf <- sweep( #4. Subtract log2 norm factor column-by-column (MARGIN = 2)
  qf,
  MARGIN = 2,
  STATS = nf_log_tot(qf, i="precursors_log"), #2.
  i = "precursors_log",
  name = "precursors_norm_tot"
)

qf <- sweep( #4. Subtract log2 norm factor column-by-column (MARGIN = 2)
  qf,
  MARGIN = 2,
  STATS = nf_log_medrat(qf, i="precursors_log"), #3.
  i = "precursors_log",
  name = "precursors_norm_mr"
)
```
:::


We explore the effect of the total normalisation in the subsequent plot. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
precursors_norm_sets <- grep("precursors_norm", names(qf), value = TRUE)
qf[, , precursors_norm_sets] |>
  longForm(colvars = c( "rep", "condition")) |> 
  data.frame() |> 
  mutate(assay = gsub(assay, pattern = "precursors_norm_", replacement = "")) |>
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  ggplot() + 
  aes(x = value,
      colour = condition,
      group = colname) +
  geom_density() +
  labs(subtitle = "Normalised log2 precursor intensities") +
  facet_wrap(~assay) +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

Visually, we do not see real changes. 

## Summarisation

Here, we will evaluate three popular summarisation methods. 

1. maxLFQ
2. median polish
3. median summarisation

1. maxLFQ summarise the precursor-level data into protein intensities using maxLFQ. 
maxLFQ first calculates all pairwise log ratio's between samples only using their shared precursors.
Particularly, it uses the median of the log ratio's between the shared precursors s, i.e. 
$$r_{ij} = median(y_{sj}-y_{si})$$.  
Hence, it first eliminates peptide effects. 
It then estimates the summaries by solving 

$$
\sum_i\sum_j(y^{prot}_j - y^{prot}_i - r_ij)^2
$$
It is implemented in the `maxLFQ` function of the `iq` package. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
for (normassay in precursors_norm_sets)
{
qf <- aggregateFeatures(
  qf, i = normassay, 
  name = paste0("proteins",gsub(normassay, pattern = "precursors_norm", replacement=""),"_maxLFQ"),
  fcol = "Protein.Group", 
  fun = function(X) iq::maxLFQ(X)$estimate
)
}
```
:::


2. median polish is a simple and robust procedure proposed by the statistician John Tukey to fit an additive model for data in a two-way layout table (usually, results from a factorial experiment) of the form row effect + column effect + overall median. Median polish utilizes the medians obtained from the rows and the columns of a two-way table to iteratively calculate the row effect and column effect on the data. The results are robust to outliers, as the iterative procedure uses the medians rather than the means. Here, the row effects are the feature effects and the column effects are the sample/run effects. Particularly, following model is considered: 

$$
y_{ip} = \beta_p^\text{pre} + \beta_i^\text{samp} + \epsilon_{ip}
$$

where $y_{ip}$ is the log-normalised precursor intensity for precursor $p$
belonging to protein $P$ in sample $i$. $\beta_p^\text{pep}$ is the
average effect of precursor $p$, which account for the fact that
different precursor yield different baseline intensities (see issue 2.
above). $\beta_i^\text{samp}$ is the average effect of sample $i$. In
other words, it provides the estimated log2-transformed and normalised intensity of protein $P$ in
sample $i$ corrected for the precursor effect, which will be used as the summarised protein value.
$\epsilon_{ip}$ is the residual effect that cannot be explained by the
average sample and precursor effects. 



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
for (normassay in precursors_norm_sets)
{
qf <- aggregateFeatures(
  qf, i = normassay, 
  name = paste0("proteins",gsub(normassay, pattern = "precursors_norm", replacement=""),"_medpol"),
  fcol = "Protein.Group", 
  fun = MsCoreUtils::medianPolish,
  na.rm = TRUE
)
}
```
:::


3. We finally adopt simple median summarisation 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
for (normassay in precursors_norm_sets)
{
qf <- aggregateFeatures(
  qf, i = normassay, 
  name = paste0("proteins",gsub(normassay, pattern = "precursors_norm", replacement=""),"_median"),
  fcol = "Protein.Group", 
  fun = matrixStats::colMedians,
  na.rm = TRUE
)
}
```
:::




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
proteins_sets <- grep("proteins", names(qf), value = TRUE)
qf[, , proteins_sets] |>
    longForm(colvars = c( "rep", "condition"), rowvars = "species") |> 
  data.frame() |> 
  mutate(assay = gsub(assay, pattern = "proteins_", replacement = ""),
         norm = strsplit(assay, split = "_") |> 
           sapply(`[`, 1),
         sum = strsplit(assay, split = "_") |> 
           sapply(`[`, 2)) |>
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  ggplot() + 
  aes(x = value,
      colour = condition,
      group = colname) +
  geom_density() +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_grid(norm~sum) +
   labs(subtitle = "Normalised log2 protein intensities")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The data processing is complete.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(qf)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Protein Normalised

We will use the same procedure as before, but we will now use the Precursor.Normalised values from DIA-NN. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
(qf_ndiann <- readQFeatures(assayData = precursors, 
                             colData = annot,
                             quantCols = "Precursor.Normalised",
                             runCol = "Run",
                             fnames = "Precursor.Id"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 18 sets:

 [1] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP1: SummarizedExperiment with 121273 rows and 1 columns 
 [2] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP2: SummarizedExperiment with 122253 rows and 1 columns 
 [3] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP3: SummarizedExperiment with 123044 rows and 1 columns 
 ...
 [16] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP4: SummarizedExperiment with 111527 rows and 1 columns 
 [17] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP5: SummarizedExperiment with 111506 rows and 1 columns 
 [18] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP6: SummarizedExperiment with 111405 rows and 1 columns 
```


:::
:::


# Data preprocessing{#sec-basic_preprocess}

The data preprocessing workflow for DIA data is similar to the workflow for DDA-LFQ data, but there are suble differences as we start from precursor level data and have additional columns. 

## Encoding missing values



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf_ndiann <- zeroIsNA(qf_ndiann, names(qf_ndiann))
```
:::


## Precursor Filtering 

Filtering removes low-quality and unreliable precursors that would otherwise introduce noise and artefacts in the data. 

### Remove questionable identifications


We apply standard filtering advised by DIA-NN: 

1. q-value threshold of 0.01 for the identification of precursors (`Q.Value`) and protein groups (`PG.Q.Value`). If the file is processed via matching between runs, it is also useful to filter on Lib.Q.Value and Lib.PG.Q.Value.

2. Remove precursors that could not be mapped, i.e. when `Precursor.Id` column is an empty string.  

3. Filter decoys, i.e. only keep precursors for which the `Decoy` column equals 0. (Note, that the `Decoy` column is not present in the output of older versions of DIA-NN)

4. Keeping only proteotypic peptides, which map uniquely to a specific protein.



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf_ndiann <-  qf_ndiann |>
  filterFeatures(~ Q.Value <= 0.01 & #1.
                     PG.Q.Value <= 0.01 &  #1.
                     Lib.Q.Value <= 0.01 & #1.
                     Lib.PG.Q.Value <= 0.01 & #1.
                     Precursor.Id != "" & #2.
                     Decoy == 0 & #3. (Note, that this filter is not available with previous versions of DIA-NN, as the report.tsv file did not include a Decoy column. So other strategies are needed if Decoys are in the output file.)
                     Proteotypic == 1) #4.
```
:::



Note, that it is important that the filtering criteria are not distorting the distribution of the test statistics in the downstream analysis for features that are non-DA. 
It can be shown that filtering will not induce bias results when the filtering criterion is independent of test statistic. The criteria that we proposed above are all based on the results of the identification step, hence, they are independent of the downstream test statistics that will be used to prioritize DA proteins. 

### Assay joining

Up to now, the data from different runs were kept in separate assays. We can now join the normalised sets into an precursor set using joinAssays(). Sets are joined by stacking the columns (samples) in a matrix and rows (features) are matched according to a row identifier, here the `Precursor.Id`.
We will store the result in the assay with name: `precursor`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
(qf_ndiann <- joinAssays(
  x = qf_ndiann,
  i = names(qf_ndiann),
  fcol = "Precursor.Id",
  name = "precursors"
  ))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 19 sets:

 [1] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP1: SummarizedExperiment with 114429 rows and 1 columns 
 [2] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP2: SummarizedExperiment with 115386 rows and 1 columns

 [19] precursors: SummarizedExperiment with 143938 rows and 18 columns 
```


:::
:::


## Log-transformation 

We perform log2-transformation with `logTransform()` from the
`QFeatures` package. We use `base = 2` and store the result in a new summarized experiment named `precursors_log`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf_ndiann <- logTransform(qf_ndiann, 
                            base = 2, 
                            i = "precursors", 
                            name = "precursors_log")
```
:::


We check the distribution. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf_ndiann[, , "precursors_log"] |> #1.
  longForm(colvars = c( "rep", "condition")) |>  #2. 
  data.frame() |> 
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  ggplot() + #3.
  aes(x = value,
      colour = condition,
      group = colname) +
  geom_density() +
  theme_minimal()
```

![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-30-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf_ndiann[, , "precursors_log"] |> #1.
  longForm(colvars = c( "rep", "condition"), rowvars = "species") |>  #2. 
  data.frame() |> 
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  ggplot() + #3.
  aes(x = value,
      colour = condition,
      group = colname) +
  geom_density() +
  theme_minimal() + 
  facet_wrap(~species)
```

## Summarisation 

We summarise according to 
1. MaxLFQ
2. Median Polish
3. Median 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf_ndiann <- aggregateFeatures(
  qf_ndiann, i = "precursors_log", 
  name = paste0("proteins_ndiann_maxLFQ"),
  fcol = "Protein.Group", 
  fun =   function(X) iq::maxLFQ(X)$estimate
)


qf_ndiann <- aggregateFeatures(
  qf_ndiann, i = "precursors_log", 
  name = paste0("proteins_ndiann_medpol"),
  fcol = "Protein.Group", 
  fun = MsCoreUtils::medianPolish,
  na.rm = TRUE
)
  
qf_ndiann <- aggregateFeatures(
  qf_ndiann, i = "precursors_log", 
  name = paste0("proteins_ndiann_median"),
  fcol = "Protein.Group", 
  fun = matrixStats::colMedians,
  na.rm = TRUE
)
```
:::


# DIA-NN protein groups 

We will now make a QFeatures object starting from the protein level summaries from DIA-NN.
Normally we would start from the protein groups file that is provided by DIA-NN, i.e. the pg_matrix.tsv. 
This was not available from the files deposited by the authors, so we extract them from the precursor table.

1. We filter the precursor file to remove features for which the identification is questionable
2. We select the variables "Run", "Protein.Group", "Protein.Names" and "PG.MaxLFQ"
3. We keep rows with unique values for these variables. Note, that all precursors from the same protein group will have the same PG.MaxLFQ value.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
proteins <- precursors |> 
  filter(Q.Value <= 0.01 & PG.Q.Value <= 0.01 & Lib.Q.Value <= 0.01 & Lib.PG.Q.Value <= 0.01) |> 
  select("Run","Protein.Group", "Protein.Names","PG.MaxLFQ") |> 
  unique()
```
:::


We add species information. Possible for this specific dataset, which is a benchmark dataset with lysate from 3 different species. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
proteins <- proteins |> 
  mutate(species = Protein.Names |> 
           strsplit(split="_") |> 
           sapply(`[`, 2) |>
            substr(start=1,stop=5))
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
  proteins |> 
  pull(species) |>
  table()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

 ECOLI  HUMAN  YEAST 
 18214 117635  42213 
```


:::
:::


We only keep ECOLI, HUMAN and YEAST. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
proteins <- proteins |> 
  filter(species %in% c("ECOLI", "HUMAN", "YEAST"))
proteins |> 
  pull(species) |>
  table()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

 ECOLI  HUMAN  YEAST 
 18214 117635  42213 
```


:::
:::


We now import the data as a qfeatures object. Note, that we do not have to reconstruct the annotation data as we already did this for the precursor table.  


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
(qf_pdiann <- readQFeatures(assayData = proteins, 
                             colData = annot,
                             quantCols = "PG.MaxLFQ",
                             runCol = "Run",
                             fnames = "Protein.Group"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 18 sets:

 [1] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP1: SummarizedExperiment with 10297 rows and 1 columns 
 [2] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP2: SummarizedExperiment with 10284 rows and 1 columns 
 [3] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP3: SummarizedExperiment with 10322 rows and 1 columns 
 ...
 [16] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP4: SummarizedExperiment with 8990 rows and 1 columns 
 [17] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP5: SummarizedExperiment with 8992 rows and 1 columns 
 [18] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP6: SummarizedExperiment with 9025 rows and 1 columns 
```


:::
:::


We join the assays. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
(qf_pdiann <- joinAssays(
  x = qf_pdiann,
  i = names(qf_pdiann),
  fcol = "Protein.Group",
  name = "proteins"
  ))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 19 sets:

 [1] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP1: SummarizedExperiment with 10297 rows and 1 columns

 [3] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_A_REP3: SummarizedExperiment with 10322 rows and 1 columns 
 ...
 [17] LFQ_Astral_DIA_15min_50ng_Condition_C_REP5: SummarizedExperiment with 8992 rows and 1 columns

 [19] proteins: SummarizedExperiment with 11722 rows and 18 columns 
```


:::
:::


All information is contained in the joined assay, so we can remove the 18 original assays. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
(qf_pdiann <- qf_pdiann[,,"proteins"])
```

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 1 set:

 [1] proteins: SummarizedExperiment with 11722 rows and 18 columns 
```


:::

## log-transformation 

We first convert zero's to NA and then we perform the log transformation. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
qf_pdiann <- zeroIsNA(qf_pdiann, i = names(qf_pdiann))
(
  qf_pdiann <- logTransform(qf_pdiann,  base = 2 , i="proteins" , name = "proteins_log")
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
An instance of class QFeatures (type: bulk) with 2 sets:

 [1] proteins: SummarizedExperiment with 11722 rows and 18 columns 
 [2] proteins_log: SummarizedExperiment with 11722 rows and 18 columns 
```


:::

# Data Modeling  and Inference 

Setup model and contrasts of interest. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
model <- ~ condition

vd <- ExploreModelMatrix::VisualizeDesign(
    sampleData =  colData(qf),
    designFormula = model,
    textSizeFitted = 4
)
vd$plotlist
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[[1]]
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-43-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
(allHypotheses <- createPairwiseContrasts(
  model, colData(qf), "condition"
  )
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "conditionC - conditionB = 0" "conditionB = 0"             
[3] "conditionC = 0"             
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
(L <- makeContrast(
  allHypotheses,
  parameterNames = colnames(vd$designmatrix)
))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
            conditionC - conditionB conditionB conditionC
(Intercept)                       0          0          0
conditionB                       -1          1          0
conditionC                        1          0          1
```


:::
:::


Fit models and do inference for all methods and quantities ("Protein.Quantified", "Protein.Normalised", "PG.MaxLFQ"). 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
for (i in proteins_sets)
  qf <- msqrob(
    qf,  
    i = i,
    formula = model,
    robust = TRUE) |> 
  hypothesisTest(i = i, contrast = L)

proteins_sets_ndiann <- grep(pattern = "proteins", names(qf_ndiann), value = TRUE)
for (i in proteins_sets_ndiann)
  qf_ndiann <- msqrob(
    qf_ndiann,  
    i = i,
    formula = model,
    robust = TRUE) |> 
  hypothesisTest(i = i, contrast = L)


  qf_pdiann <- msqrob(
    qf_pdiann,  
    i = "proteins_log",
    formula = model,
    robust = TRUE) |> 
  hypothesisTest(i = "proteins_log", contrast = L)
```
:::


We extract all results tables


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
inferences <- lapply(proteins_sets, function(i) {
  msqrobCollect(qf[[i]], L) |>
    mutate(species =  rep(rowData(qf[[i]])$species, ncol(L)), 
           method = i |> gsub(pattern="proteins_",replacement =""),
           norm = strsplit(method, split = "_") |> 
             sapply(`[`, 1),
           sum = strsplit(method, split = "_") |> 
             sapply(`[`, 2))
           }
  )

inferences_ndiann <- lapply(proteins_sets_ndiann, function(i) {
  msqrobCollect(qf_ndiann[[i]], L) |>
    mutate(species =  rep(rowData(qf_ndiann[[i]])$species, ncol(L)), 
           method = i |> gsub(pattern="proteins_",replacement =""),
           norm = strsplit(method, split = "_") |> 
             sapply(`[`, 1),
           sum = strsplit(method, split = "_") |> 
             sapply(`[`, 2))
           }
  )

inferences_pdiann <- 
  msqrobCollect(qf_pdiann[["proteins_log"]], L) |>
    mutate(species =  rep(rowData(qf_pdiann[["proteins_log"]])$species, ncol(L)), 
           method = "ndiann_pdiann",
           norm = strsplit(method, split = "_") |> 
             sapply(`[`, 1),
           sum = strsplit(method, split = "_") |> 
             sapply(`[`, 2))

inferences <-  rbind(
  do.call(rbind, inferences), 
  do.call(rbind, inferences_ndiann),
  inferences_pdiann)
```
:::



# Assess performance

Note, that the evaluations in this section are typically not possible on real experimental data. 
Indeed, we are using a spike-in dataset so we know the ground thruth: all human UPS proteins are differentially abundant (DA) between the conditions and the yeast proteins are non-DA.

We use a nominal FDR level of 0.05


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
alpha <- 0.05
```
:::


## Real Fold changes 

As this is a spike-in study with known ground truth, we can also plot the log2 fold change distributions against the expected values, in this case 0 for the yeast proteins and the difference of the log concentration for the spiked-in UPS standards. We first create a small table with the real values.

1. We extract the levels from factor condition
2. We convert then into a number as they refer to the real ratio
3. We log2-transform them 
4. We calculate all pairwise differences between log2 transformed ratio's to obtain the log2 fold changes

We use a similar operation to construct the name of the corresponding contrast.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
conc <- data.frame(species = c("ECOLI", "YEAST", "HUMAN"), conditionA = c(5,30,65), conditionB= c(20,15,65), conditionC = c(32,3,65))
realLogFC <- data.frame(
  species = c("ECOLI", "YEAST", "HUMAN"), 
  "conditionC - conditionB" = log2(conc$conditionC) - log2(conc$conditionB),
  "conditionB" = log2(conc$conditionB) -log2(conc$conditionA),
  "conditionC" = log2(conc$conditionC) -log2(conc$conditionA)
)

realLogFC  <- realLogFC |> pivot_longer(!species, names_to = "contrast", values_to = "logFC") |> data.frame() |> mutate(contrast =  contrast |> recode("conditionC...conditionB" = "conditionC - conditionB"))
```
:::


## True and false positives

All human UPS proteins are differentially abundant (DA) between the conditions and the yeast proteins are non-DA.
We make a new variable spikein in the inference_list. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tpFpTable <- group_by(inferences, norm, sum, contrast) |>
    filter(adjPval < alpha) |>
    summarise("TP" = sum(species != "HUMAN"),
              "FP" = sum(species == "HUMAN"),
              "FDP" = mean(species == "HUMAN")) 
```
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# colors <- c(
#   maxLFQ = "#fcba03",
#   medpol = "#c29310",
#   median = "#423512"
# )

tpFpTable |> pivot_longer(
  cols = c("TP", "FP"),
  names_to = "metric", values_to = "count") |>
  ggplot() +
  aes(
    x = norm, y = count,
    fill = sum
  ) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity", position = position_dodge(preserve = "single"),
    color = "black"
  ) +
  facet_grid(contrast ~ metric, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal() +
#  scale_fill_manual(values = colors, breaks = names(colors)) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-49-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## TPR - FDP curves

We generate the TPR-FDP curves to assess the performance of the different workflows to prioritise differentially abundant proteins. Again, these curves are built using the ground truth information about which proteins are differentially abundant (spiked in) and which proteins are constant across samples. We create two functions to compute the TPR and the FDP.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
computeFDP <- function(pval, tp) {
    ord <- order(pval)
    fdp <- cumsum(!tp[ord]) / 1:length(tp)
    fdp[order(ord)]
}
computeTPR <- function(pval, tp, nTP = NULL) {
    if (is.null(nTP)) nTP <- sum(tp)
    ord <- order(pval)
    tpr <- cumsum(tp[ord]) / nTP
    tpr[order(ord)]
}
```
:::


We apply these functions and compute the corresponding metric using the statistical inference results and the ground truth information.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
performance <- inferences |> group_by(norm, sum, contrast) |> 
    na.exclude() |>
    mutate(tpr = computeTPR(pval, species != "HUMAN"),
           fdp = computeFDP(pval, species != "HUMAN")) |>
    arrange(fdp)
```
:::


We also highlight the observed FDP at a $alpha = 5\%$ FDR threshold.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
workPoints <- performance |> 
  group_by(sum, norm, contrast) |>
    filter(adjPval < 0.05) |>
    slice_max(pval)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(performance) +
    aes(
        y = fdp,
        x = tpr,
        color = norm
    ) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point(data = workPoints, size = 3) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.05, linetype = 2) +
    facet_grid(sum ~ contrast) +
    coord_flip(ylim = c(0, 0.2)) +
    theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](02-workflow-optimisation-astral_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-53-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

 
## volcano-plots

We make a separate plot per contrast and compare the volcano plots for all normalisation x summarisation combinations.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
for (i in unique(inferences$contrast))
{
print(
  inferences |> 
  filter(contrast == i) |> 
  plot_volcano() + 
  facet_grid(sum~norm) +
    aes(color = species, shape = adjPval < alpha) + 
   scale_color_manual( 
     values = c("firebrick","grey20", "#619CFF"), #3. 
     name = "",
     labels = c("ECOLI","HUMAN","YEAST")
     ) +
  labs(shape="FDR < 0.05", subtitle = i)  +
    geom_vline(aes(xintercept = logFC, col = species), data = realLogFC |> filter(contrast == i))
#  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = -log10(adjAlpha)), data = inferences |> 
#               filter(contrast == i) |> 
#               group_by(sum,norm) |> 
#               summarize(adjAlpha = alpha *mean(adjPval < alpha, na.rm=TRUE), .groups="drop"))
)
}
```

# Discussion 

Note, that normalisation is often detrimental. 
This is because we have an extremely clean dataset where the same protein mass was available in all samples.  
Moreover, spike-in datasets and mixture datasets often impose a burden on the normalisation. 

Note, that DIA-NN normalisation can handle this quite well. It consists of global normalisation followed by rention time normalisation. So each entry has its own normalisation factor. 

Surprisingly, median summarisation performs quite well here.
